question_text: When an electric field is applied across semiconductor electrons move from lower energy level to higher energy level in the conduction band. electrons move from a higher energy level to lower energy level in the conduction band. holes in the valence band move from higher energy level to lower energy level. holes in the valence band move from lower energy level to higher energy level.
question_html:
When an electric field is applied across semiconductor

  1. electrons move from lower energy level to higher energy level in the conduction band.
  2. electrons move from a higher energy level to lower energy level in the conduction band.
  3. holes in the valence band move from higher energy level to lower energy level.
  4. holes in the valence band move from lower energy level to higher energy level.
solution_text: (a, c) In the direction of electric field the P.E or energy of electric field decrease. So electrons always move opposite to direction of E.F., i.e. from lower energy level to higher energy level and holes move from higher energy level to lower energy level. In semiconductors, electrons are in conduction band and holes are in valence band.

(a, c) In the direction of electric field the P.E or energy of electric field decrease.
So electrons always move opposite to direction of E.F., i.e. from lower energy level to higher energy level and holes move from higher energy level to lower energy level. In semiconductors, electrons are in conduction band and holes are in valence band.

question_text: Consider an NPN transistor with its base-emitter junction forward biased and collector-base junction reverse biased. Which of the following statements are true? Electrons crossover from emitter to collector. Holes move from base to collector. Electrons move from emitter to base. Electrons from emitter move out of base without going to the collector.
question_html:
Consider an NPN transistor with its base-emitter junction forward biased and collector-base junction reverse biased. Which of the following statements are true?

  1. Electrons crossover from emitter to collector.
  2. Holes move from base to collector.
  3. Electrons move from emitter to base.
  4. Electrons from emitter move out of base without going to the collector.
solution_text: Electrons are repelled by forward bias from emitter to collector {tex}\cong{/tex} 5 % of electrons combine with holes of base and rest 95 % electrons of emitter are attracted by reverse bias of collector-base junction. So electrons move from emitter to collector through base i.e. emitter to base verifies (a, c).
solution_html:
Electrons are repelled by forward bias from emitter to collector {tex}\cong{/tex} 5 % of electrons combine with holes of base and rest 95 % electrons of emitter are attracted by reverse bias of collector-base junction.

So electrons move from emitter to collector through base i.e. emitter to base verifies (a, c).

book: Class 12 - Physics - Exemplar Book by NCERT
question_text: Figure shows the transfer characteristics of a base biased CE transistor. Which of the following statements are true? At V i = 0.4V, transistor is in active state. At V i = 1V, it can be used as an amplifier. At V i = 0.5V, it can be used as a switch turned off. At V i = 2.5V, it can be used as a switch turned on.
question_html:
Figure shows the transfer characteristics of a base biased CE transistor. Which of the following statements are true?

  1. At Vi = 0.4V, transistor is in active state.
  2. At Vi = 1V, it can be used as an amplifier.
  3. At Vi = 0.5V, it can be used as a switch turned off.
  4. At Vi = 2.5V, it can be used as a switch turned on.
solution_text: At V i = 0.4 V there is no collector current only output voltage is V 0 due to V CE battery. So at 0.4 V transistor is not in active mode. When at V i = 1 volt, the transistor is between active region (0.6 V to 2 V). So it can be used as an amplifier. At V i = 0.5 V, the transistor is in cut-off state and it can be used as a switch turned off. At V i = 2.5 V, the transistor is beyond the active region. The collector current is in a saturated state and there is no effect on changing input voltage and the transistor can be used as a switch turned on. So, the statements (b), (c) and (d) are true.
solution_html:
1. At Vi = 0.4 V there is no collector current only output voltage is V0 due to VCE battery. So at 0.4 V transistor is not in active mode.
  2. When at Vi = 1 volt, the transistor is between active region (0.6 V to 2 V). So it can be used as an amplifier.
  3. At Vi = 0.5 V, the transistor is in cut-off state and it can be used as a switch turned off.
  4. At Vi = 2.5 V, the transistor is beyond the active region. The collector current is in a saturated state and there is no effect on changing input voltage and the transistor can be used as a switch turned on.
    So, the statements (b), (c) and (d) are true.

question_text: In an NPN transistor circuit, the collector current is 10mA. If 95 percent of the electrons emitted reaches the collector, which of the following statements are true? The emitter current will be 8 mA. The emitter current will be 10.53 mA. The base current will be 0.53 mA. The base current will be 2 mA.
question_html:
In an NPN transistor circuit, the collector current is 10mA. If 95 percent of the electrons emitted reaches the collector, which of the following statements are true?

  1. The emitter current will be 8 mA.
  2. The emitter current will be 10.53 mA.
  3. The base current will be 0.53 mA.
  4. The base current will be 2 mA.
solution_text: (b, c) I c = 10 mA I c = 95 % of I e or I c = {tex}\frac{95}{100} I_e{/tex} I e = {tex}\frac{10 m A \times 100}{95}{/tex} = 10.53 mA I b + I c = I e So I b = I e - I c = 10.53 - 10 = 0.53 mA
solution_html:
(b, c)
Ic = 10 mA
Ic = 95 % of Ie or Ic = {tex}\frac{95}{100} I_e{/tex}
Ie = {tex}\frac{10 m A \times 100}{95}{/tex} = 10.53 mA
Ib + Ic = Ie
So Ib = Ie - Ic = 10.53 - 10 = 0.53 mA

question_html:
In the depletion region of a diode,

  1. there are no mobile charges.
  2. the equal number of holes and electrons exist, making the region neutral.
  3. recombination of holes and electrons has taken place.
  4. immobile charged ions exist.
solution_text: (a, b, c and d) During the formation of p-n junction electrons from n side and holes from p side move towards each other and form a potential barrier across the junction. Within the barrier, there are holes and electrons or ions which cannot move. so in depletion layer no mobile charges but there are negative and positive ion; barrier layer was formed by recombination of electrons hand holes when pn junction formed.

(a, b, c and d) During the formation of p-n junction electrons from n side and holes from p side move towards each other and form a potential barrier across the junction. Within the barrier, there are holes and electrons or ions which cannot move.
so in depletion layer no mobile charges but there are negative and positive ion; barrier layer was formed by recombination of electrons hand holes when pn junction formed.

question_text: The breakdown in a reverse-biased p–n junction diode is more likely to occur due to the large velocity of the minority charge carriers if the doping concentration is small. the large velocity of the minority charge carriers if the doping concentration is large. the strong electric field in a depletion region if the doping concentration is small. the strong electric field in the depletion region if the doping concentration is large.
question_html:
The breakdown in a reverse-biased p–n junction diode is more likely to occur due to

  1. the large velocity of the minority charge carriers if the doping concentration is small.
  2. the large velocity of the minority charge carriers if the doping concentration is large.
  3. the strong electric field in a depletion region if the doping concentration is small.
  4. the strong electric field in the depletion region if the doping concentration is large.
solution_text: (a, d) In reverse biasing, minority charge carriers accelerate due to a high electric field applied (due to reverse P.D.). So the minority charge carrier in both junction accelerated toward depletion layer, which on striking with atoms causes ionization of atoms resulting from the secondary electron and so more number of charge carriers constitute more current in the reverse direction. More current in the thin depletion layer is due to the heavy doping and large no. of ions in the depletion layer which give rise to the strong electric field across the junction.
solution_html:
(a, d)
In reverse biasing, minority charge carriers accelerate due to a high electric field applied (due to reverse P.D.). So the minority charge carrier in both junction accelerated toward depletion layer, which on striking with atoms causes ionization of atoms resulting from the secondary electron and so more number of charge carriers constitute more current in the reverse direction.
More current in the thin depletion layer is due to the heavy doping and large no. of ions in the depletion layer which give rise to the strong electric field across the junction.

question_text: Why are elemental dopants for Silicon or Germanium is usually chosen from group XIII or group XV?
question_html: Why are elemental dopants for Silicon or Germanium is usually chosen from group XIII or group XV?
solution_text: The size of the dopant atom should be equivalent to the size of Si or Ge. So that the symmetry of pure Si or Ge, does not disturb and dopants can contribute the charge carrier on forming covalent bonds with Si or Germanium atoms. As the silicon and germanium belongs to XIVth group so similar size of atom will be in XIII and XV group of modern periodic table.

question_text: Sn, C, and Si, Ge are all group XIV elements. Yet, Sn is a conductor, C is an insulator while Si and Ge are semiconductors. Why?
question_html: Sn, C, and Si, Ge are all group XIV elements. Yet, Sn is a conductor, C is an insulator while Si and Ge are semiconductors. Why?
solution_text: A material will conduct current if there is no energy gap between conduction and valence band in energy band diagram of atom. This energy gap decreases from insulator to semiconductor and form semiconductor and from semiconductor to conductor. The energy gaps in Sn, C, Si and Ge are 0 eV, 0.54 eV, 1.1 eV and 0.7 eV respectively related to their atomic size. So the Sn is a conductor, C is an insulator while Si and Ge are semiconductors.
solution_html:
A material will conduct current if there is no energy gap between conduction and valence band in energy band diagram of atom. This energy gap decreases from insulator to semiconductor and form semiconductor and from semiconductor to conductor.
The energy gaps in Sn, C, Si and Ge are 0 eV, 0.54 eV, 1.1 eV and 0.7 eV respectively related to their atomic size. So the Sn is a conductor, C is an insulator while Si and Ge are semiconductors.

question_text: The amplifiers X, Y, and Z are connected in series. If the voltage gains of X, Y and Z are 10, 20 and 30, respectively and the input signal is 1 mV peak value, then what is the output signal voltage (peak value) if dc supply voltage is 10V? if dc supply voltage is 5V?
question_html:
The amplifiers X, Y, and Z are connected in series. If the voltage gains of X, Y and Z are 10, 20 and 30, respectively and the input signal is 1 mV peak value, then what is the output signal voltage (peak value)

  1. if dc supply voltage is 10V?
  2. if dc supply voltage is 5V?
solution_text: {tex}A_{V_{x}}=10, A_{V_{y}}=20 \text { and } A_{V_{z}}=30 {/tex} {tex}\Delta V_{i}{/tex} = 1mV = 10 -3 V {tex}\frac{\text { Output signal voltage }\left(\Delta V_{0}\right)}{\text { Input signal voltage }\left(\Delta V_{i}\right)}{/tex} = total voltage amplification = {tex}A_{v_{x}} \times A_{v_{y}} \times A_{v_{z_{z}}}{/tex} or {tex}\Delta V_{0}=A_{v_{x}} \times A_{v_{y}} \times A_{v_{z}} \times \Delta V i{/tex} = 10 {tex}\times 20 \times 30 \times 10^{-3}{/tex} = 6V If d.c. supply voltage is 10V, then output is 6V since theretical gain is equal to practial gain, i.e., output can never be greater than 6V. Hence V 0 = 6V If d.c. supply voltage is 5V, i.e. V cc = 5V, then output peak will not exceed 5V. Hence, the output voltage is given by V 0 = 5V
solution_html:
{tex}A_{V_{x}}=10, A_{V_{y}}=20 \text { and } A_{V_{z}}=30 {/tex}
{tex}\Delta V_{i}{/tex} = 1mV = 10-3V
{tex}\frac{\text { Output signal voltage }\left(\Delta V_{0}\right)}{\text { Input signal voltage }\left(\Delta V_{i}\right)}{/tex} = total voltage amplification
= {tex}A_{v_{x}} \times A_{v_{y}} \times A_{v_{z_{z}}}{/tex}
or {tex}\Delta V_{0}=A_{v_{x}} \times A_{v_{y}} \times A_{v_{z}} \times \Delta V i{/tex} = 10 {tex}\times 20 \times 30 \times 10^{-3}{/tex} = 6V

  1. If d.c. supply voltage is 10V, then output is 6V since theretical gain is equal to practial gain, i.e., output can never be greater than 6V. Hence V0 = 6V
  2. If d.c. supply voltage is 5V, i.e. Vcc = 5V, then output peak will not exceed 5V. Hence, the output voltage is given by V0 = 5V

question_text: In a CE transistor amplifier, there is a current and voltage gain associated with the circuit. In other words, there is a power gain. Considering power a measure of energy, does the circuit violate the conservation of energy?
question_html: In a CE transistor amplifier, there is a current and voltage gain associated with the circuit. In other words, there is a power gain. Considering power a measure of energy, does the circuit violate the conservation of energy?
solution_text: In the CE transistor amplifier, the DC supply is connected to give the energy to signal. Due to this fact, there is a large power gain in the CE configuration amplifier. So, the energy of the output signal is equal to the sum of energy of signal (low) and energy supplied by DC source in output CE circuit (V ce ). Hence ,the circuit doesn't violate the conservation of energy.
solution_html:
In the CE transistor amplifier, the DC supply is connected to give the energy to signal. Due to this fact, there is a large power gain in the CE configuration amplifier.
So, the energy of the output signal is equal to the sum of energy of signal (low) and energy supplied by DC source in output CE circuit (Vce). Hence ,the circuit doesn't violate the conservation of energy.

question_text: Name the type of a diode whose characteristics are shown in Fig. (a) and Fig. (b). What does the point P in Fig. (a) represent? What does the points P and Q in Fig. (b) represent?
question_html:
1. Name the type of a diode whose characteristics are shown in Fig. (a) and Fig. (b).

  2. What does the point P in Fig. (a) represent?
  3. What does the points P and Q in Fig. (b) represent?
solution_text: Diode in figure (a) is Zener diode figure (b) shows solar cell. Zener breakdown voltage is represented by point P in figure (a). In figure (b), Q represents zero voltage and negative current(short circuit current). It means that light energy falling on a solar cell with at least minimum threshold frequency gives the current in the opposite direction to that due to a battery connected to solar cell. But for point P the battery is short-circuited and hence represents the short-circuited current. In figure (b), Point P represents some positive voltage on the solar cells with zero current through the solar cell.(open circuit voltage) So there is battery connected to a solar cell which gives rise to the equal and opposite current to that in solar cell by virtue of light falling on it. As current is zero for point P, hence we say P represents open-circuit voltage.
solution_html:
1. Diode in figure (a) is Zener diode figure (b) shows solar cell.
  2. Zener breakdown voltage is represented by point P in figure (a).
  3. In figure (b), Q represents zero voltage and negative current(short circuit current). It means that light energy falling on a solar cell with at least minimum threshold frequency gives the current in the opposite direction to that due to a battery connected to solar cell. But for point P the battery is short-circuited and hence represents the short-circuited current.
    In figure (b), Point P represents some positive voltage on the solar cells with zero current through the solar cell.(open circuit voltage)
    So there is battery connected to a solar cell which gives rise to the equal and opposite current to that in solar cell by virtue of light falling on it.
    As current is zero for point P, hence we say P represents open-circuit voltage.

question_html: Three photo diodes D1, D2 and D3 are made of semiconductors having band gaps of 2.5eV, 2eV and 3eV, respectively. Which ones will be able to detect light of wavelength 6000 {tex}\mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} {/tex}?
solution_text: {tex}\lambda=6000 \mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} =6000 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}{/tex} {tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{6000 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} e V{/tex} {tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 3 \times 10^{-34+8+10+19}}{6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{3}} e V=\frac{3.31 \times 10^{-34+37}}{1.60 \times 10^{3}}{/tex} {tex}=\frac{331}{160} \times \frac{10^{3}}{10^{3}} e V=2.06 e V{/tex} For the incident radiation to be detected by the photodiode, energy of incident radiation photon should be greater than the band gap. This is true only for D2. Therefore, only D2 will detect this radiation.
solution_html:
{tex}\lambda=6000 \mathop {\text{A}}\limits^{\text{o}} =6000 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{m}{/tex}
{tex}E=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{6.62 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^{8}}{6000 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} e V{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{6.62 \times 3 \times 10^{-34+8+10+19}}{6 \times 1.6 \times 10^{3}} e V=\frac{3.31 \times 10^{-34+37}}{1.60 \times 10^{3}}{/tex}
{tex}=\frac{331}{160} \times \frac{10^{3}}{10^{3}} e V=2.06 e V{/tex}
For the incident radiation to be detected by the photodiode, energy of incident radiation photon should be greater than the band gap. This is true only for D2. Therefore, only D2 will detect this radiation.

question_text: How would you set up a circuit to obtain NOT gate using a transistor?
question_html: How would you set up a circuit to obtain NOT gate using a transistor?
solution_text: The NOT gate is a device which has only one input and one output i.e., {tex}\bar{A}{/tex} = Y means, Y equals to NOT A. Thus, A Y = {tex}\bar{A}{/tex} 0 1 1 0 Here the base B of the transistor is connected to the input A through the input resistance R B and the emitter is earthed. The collector is connected to a 5V battery. The output Y is the voltage at C w.r.t. earth. The resistance R B and R C are so chosen that if the emitter-base junction is unbiased, the transistor is in the cut-off region and if the emitter-base junction is in forwarding bias by 5V input at A, the transistor is in saturation state.
solution_html:
The NOT gate is a device which has only one input and one output i.e., {tex}\bar{A}{/tex} = Y means, Y equals to NOT A. Thus,

A Y = {tex}\bar{A}{/tex}
0 1
1 0

Here the base B of the transistor is connected to the input A through the input resistance RB and the emitter is earthed. The collector is connected to a 5V battery.
The output Y is the voltage at C w.r.t. earth.

The resistance RB and RC are so chosen that if the emitter-base junction is unbiased, the transistor is in the cut-off region and if the emitter-base junction is in forwarding bias by 5V input at A, the transistor is in saturation state.

question_text: Consider the circuit arrangement shown in Figure. (a) for studying input and output characteristics of npn transistor in CE configuration. Select the values of RB and RC for a transistor whose V BE = 0.7 V, so that the transistor is operating at point Q as shown in the characteristics shown in Figure (b). Given that the input impedance of the transistor is very small and V CC = V BB = 16 V, also find the voltage gain and power gain of circuit making appropriate assumptions.
question_html:
Consider the circuit arrangement shown in Figure. (a) for studying input and output characteristics of npn transistor in CE configuration.

Select the values of RB and RC for a transistor whose VBE = 0.7 V, so that the transistor is operating at point Q as shown in the characteristics shown in Figure (b).

Given that the input impedance of the transistor is very small and VCC = VBB = 16 V, also find the voltage gain and power gain of circuit making appropriate assumptions.
solution_text: Circuit diagram: Using K VL at loop 1, R B i B + V BE = V BB R B = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{BB}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{BE}}}{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{B}}}{/tex} Putting the value from the curve, R B = {tex}\frac{16-0.7}{30} \times{/tex} 10 6 ohms R B = 510K {tex}\Omega{/tex} Using K VL in loop 2, R C i C + V C E = V CC R C = {tex}\frac{V_{c c}-V_{c E}}{i_{c}}{/tex} Putting the values from the curve, R C = {tex}\frac{16-8}{4} \times{/tex} 10 3 ohms R C = 2K {tex}\Omega{/tex} The voltage gain will be given by ;- A V = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}}{/tex} A V = -0.522 The power gain will be given by :- A P = {tex}\left(\frac{i_{c}}{i_{B}}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{R_{C}}{R_{B}}\right){/tex} A P = 69
solution_html:
Circuit diagram:

Using KVL at loop 1,
RBiB + VBE = VBB
RB = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{BB}}-\mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{BE}}}{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{B}}}{/tex}
Putting the value from the curve,
RB =  {tex}\frac{16-0.7}{30} \times{/tex} 106 ohms
RB = 510K{tex}\Omega{/tex}
Using KVL in loop 2,
RCiC + VCE = VCC
RC = {tex}\frac{V_{c c}-V_{c E}}{i_{c}}{/tex}
Putting the values from the curve,
RC = {tex}\frac{16-8}{4} \times{/tex}103 ohms
RC = 2K{tex}\Omega{/tex}
The voltage gain will be given by ;-
AV = {tex}-\frac{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{c}} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{c}}}{\mathrm{i}_{\mathrm{B}} \mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{B}}}{/tex}
AV = -0.522
The power gain will be given by :-
AP = {tex}\left(\frac{i_{c}}{i_{B}}\right)^{2}\left(\frac{R_{C}}{R_{B}}\right){/tex}
AP = 69

question_text: Assuming the ideal diode, draw the output waveform for the circuit given in the given Figure. Explain the waveform.
question_html: Assuming the ideal diode, draw the output waveform for the circuit given in the given Figure. Explain the waveform.
solution_text: When signal 20 sin {tex}ω{/tex} t gives input voltage less than 5 volt (because after 5V diode will get positive voltage at its P-junction) then diode will be in reverse bias so resistance of diode remain infinity so input signal will not pass through diode and battery path so output across A and B will increase from 0-5 V (graph OC). Now when the input voltage 20 sin ωt increase beyond 5V then path of diode 5 V battery will offer very low resistance, so the current passes through diode and battery and output (across A and B) remain 5V (graph CD). Now when the voltage decreases the diode will be in reverse bias and output will again fall from 5V to 0V as input changes (graph DE). When input voltage becomes negative (there is opposite of 5V battery in p-n junction input voltage becomes more than 5V now) the diode is in reversed bias it will not conduct current through CD, and in output across AB will get same as input AC i.e. for negative cycle diode offer infinite resistance as compared to R in series graph E, F, G. Same repetition of input and output continues-graph showing the output waveform.
solution_html:
When signal 20 sin {tex}ω{/tex}t gives input voltage less than 5 volt (because after 5V diode will get positive voltage at its P-junction) then diode will be in reverse bias so resistance of diode remain infinity so input signal will not pass through diode and battery path so output across A and B will increase from 0-5 V (graph OC).
Now when the input voltage 20 sin ωt increase beyond 5V then path of diode 5 V battery will offer very low resistance, so the current passes through diode and battery and output (across A and B) remain 5V (graph CD).
Now when the voltage decreases the diode will be in reverse bias and output will again fall from 5V to 0V as input changes (graph DE). When input voltage becomes negative (there is opposite of 5V battery in p-n junction input voltage becomes more than 5V now) the diode is in reversed bias it will not conduct current through CD, and in output across AB will get same as input AC i.e. for negative cycle diode offer infinite resistance as compared to R in series graph E, F, G.
Same repetition of input and output continues-graph showing the output waveform.

question_html:

solution_text: When As (pentavalent) is added to Si the n-type water is created. So the number of majority carriers in n-type water, N e = (N D and D Si ) = {tex}\frac{1}{10^{6}} \times 5 \times 10^{28}=5 \times 10^{22} / \mathrm{m}^{3}{/tex} For number of minority carriers n h {tex}n_{e} \cdot n_{h}=n_{i}^{2}{/tex} {tex}n_{h}=\frac{n_{i}^{2}}{n_{e}}=\frac{1.5 \times 10^{16} \times 1.5 \times 10^{16}}{5 \times 10^{22}}{/tex} (n i =1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 16 m -3 ) (Given) = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 32-22 =0.45 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 +10 m -3 When boron(trivalent) is implanted in Si crystal, p- type wafer is formed with number of holes, n h = (N D {tex}\times{/tex} n of Si) {tex}\frac{200}{10^{6}} \times 5 \times 10^{28}=1000 \times 10^{28-6}{/tex} n h = 1×10 25 per m 3 Minority carrier in p-type water n e . n h = {tex}n_i^2{/tex} {tex}n_{e}=\frac{n_{i}^{2}}{n_{h}}=\frac{1.5 \times 10^{16} \times 1.5 \times 10^{16}}{10^{25}}=2.25 \times 10^{32-25}{/tex} = 2.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 electrons per m 3 When reversed bias is applied on p-n junction then the minority charge carrier moves toward depletion layer i.e., holes nh=(0.45 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 10 per m 3 ) from n side and n e =2.25 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 7 m -3 from p side moves towards junction and make the depletion layer thicker.
solution_html:
1. When As (pentavalent) is added to Si the n-type water is created.
    So the number of majority carriers in n-type water,
    Ne = (ND and DSi) = {tex}\frac{1}{10^{6}} \times 5 \times 10^{28}=5 \times 10^{22} / \mathrm{m}^{3}{/tex}

    {tex}n_{e} \cdot n_{h}=n_{i}^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}n_{h}=\frac{n_{i}^{2}}{n_{e}}=\frac{1.5 \times 10^{16} \times 1.5 \times 10^{16}}{5 \times 10^{22}}{/tex} (ni =1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1016 m-3) (Given)
    = 0.3 {tex}\times{/tex} 1.5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1032-22=0.45 {tex}\times{/tex} 10+10 m-3
    When boron(trivalent) is implanted in Si crystal, p- type wafer is formed with number of holes,
    nh = (ND {tex}\times{/tex} n of Si)
    {tex}\frac{200}{10^{6}} \times 5 \times 10^{28}=1000 \times 10^{28-6}{/tex}

  1. when A is positive and B is negative
  2. when A is negative and B is positive
    {tex} \frac{1}{\text { slope }}=1000 \Omega{/tex}
  3. When B is negative and C is positive
  4. When B is positive and C is negative
  5. When A is positive and C is negative
  6. When A is negative and C is positive

    From these graphs of current-voltage characteristics shown in Figure (c) to (h), determine the arrangement of components between A, B and C.
